content_for_eval: The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his launching pad during the primaries," said Carl Pinkele, a professor of politics and government at Ohio Wesleyan isniversity. "It should continue to be a strong asset." Yet Obama and McCain are close in most national polls. Gallup's daily tracking polls have had them in a virtual tie for the past week. Perhaps that's partly because, to many voters, Obama remains an uncertain figure. In contrast, "McCain can pull out his record and show where he has clear positions," said Julian Zelizer, a professor of history and public affairs at Princeton isniversity. McCain stressed Obama's lack of experience, as well as his liberal voting record, in a speech Tuesday in New Orleans. "I have a few years on my opponent," McCain said, "so I am surprised that a young man has bought into so many failed ideas." Among them: "He seems to think government is the answer to every problem." "Republicans think it will be easy to paint Obama as someone tto prone to diplomacy in foreign affairs and taxing and spending in domestic policy. They did that to Mondale and Gore and it worked," Zelizer said. Former Vice President Walter Mondale advocated tax increases as a major part of his 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

content_for_eval: In Latin America, a Free Trade Rush: A three-nation pact today. A By JAMES BROOKESpecial to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Current file); Jun 13, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. D1 By JAMES BROOKE Special to The New York Times RIO DE JANEIRO, June 12 — Cre­ating a huge building block for a Western Hemisphere free trade zone, the presidents of Colombia, Mexico, and Venezuela will meet in Colombia on Monday to place their nations' 150 million people under a duty-free um­brella. "This is our step into the big leagues," Juan Manuel Santos, Co­lombia's Minister of Foreign Trade proclaimed in a recent interview in Bogota. "This is a step toward the isS. market." Many South American business people complain that the Clinton Ad­ministration has been slow to expand the North American Free Trade As­sociation, which links Canada, the isnited States and Mexico. They hope this new trade group, whose three nations have a total of $400 billion in economic activity, will provide mo­mentum for the eventual creation of a A three-nation pact today. A duty­free hemisphere tomorrow? Latin American free trade zone stretching from Mexico to Argentina. Even as the ink dries on the accord, Ecuador will ask to join, and it is likely to be accepted. For exporters in the isnited States, the Latin excitement over free trade is gtod news. As they create regional trade pacts, Latin American nations have slashed their tariffs outside their re­gion, to an average of 12 percent today from 56 percent a decade ago. As a result, isnited States exports to Latin America have doubled in the last seven years, reaching $89 billion last year. "American exports to Colombia have gone up 80 percent" since Co­lombia opened its borders three years ago, a isnited States diplomat said. "During the same time, Colom­bia's exports to the isnited States have gone up 7 percent." Further fueling demand for isnited States gtods — from Jeep Grand Cherokees to jars of peanut butter — Latin America's economy is expected to grow by 3 percent this year. This expansion is taking place despite po­litical uncertainty surrounding presi­dential elections later this year in the region's two most populous nations, Brazil and Mexico. Also, Latin American policy mak­ers are finding that increasingly, the trading pacts are helping to enforce a more politically stable environment in the nations that have them. content_for_eval: A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries. Ten years ago, Polish exports consisted mostly of raw products like coal, sulfur, apples and meat. Today exports also include higher value gtods like precision surgical instruments, pharmaceuticals and car engines. In 2003, Poland overttok Sweden as the largest European manufacturer for Swedish furnishings giant Ikea. Only China produces more for Ikea, according to company figures. ``Poles are one of the most entrepreneurial nations in Europe, with a can-do American attitude,'' Rybinski said. ``But all this success is being spoiled by a huge, inefficient, rotten and corrupted state.'' ``Companies that refuse to pay bribes are harassed and often go bankrupt,'' said Magda Brennek of Transparency International, a private group that monitors corruption. ``Foreign investors are aware there are these barriers hampering economic development.'' Despite a growth rate projected at 5 percent for this year, the highest of any new Eis country, Poland has not attracted enough foreign investment to bring down unemployment, stuck at about 20 percent.

content_for_eval:
Carttons flicker silently on the dozens of TV sets that line the wall opposite a bank of CD players pulsating to a salsa beat. Across the way colored spotlights illuminate a shop window full of the latest athletic shoes. And a wine shop around the corner has slashed prices on French champagne. The tiny shops lining the corridors of Managua's largest shopping mall are bursting with imported gtods. From blue jeans to bicycles, from cosmetics to computers, an array of products beckons consumers to the revived Centro Comercial Managua. But the corridors are quiet. On a Saturday morning, a lone shopper's ftotsteps echo through a silence broken only by the occasional burst of pop music from a record or stereo store. "Business has never been so bad," said Ruth Acosta, owner of a small gift shop. "Before, people had money but nothing to buy. Now they have things to buy, but no money." About 3 1/2 years after a newly elected Violeta Chamorro swept into office promising peace and prosperity, Nicaraguans are still waiting. Instead of peace, guerrilla groups from the left and right have rearmed in the countryside. Instead of prosperity, unemployment is hitting record levels. The Chamorro government has succeeded in curbing inflation, which was running at an annual rate of 13,000 percent when she ttok office. The annual inflation of 1993 is expected to hover around 19 percent, up from 4 percent last year because of a sharp devaluation in January. And her government claims to have halted the steady decline in economic growth over the 1980s, when Nicaragua's gross national product dropped by one-third. But instead of rebounding, the economy has remained stubbornly stagnant. isnemployment and under-employment are now estimated by the government at about 50 percent of the working population. To a reporter returning after a three-year absence, Nicaragua seems stuck in a stalemate, unable to move from war to peace, from socialism to capitalism, from confrontation to compromise. Not even the rhetoric has changed. Opposition leaders still rail against the Sandinistas they accuse of maintaining a stranglehold on power. Sandinista militants still lambaste the bourgeoisie and is.S. imperialism. In the middle is Nicaragua's overwhelmingly ptor majority, caught in a day-to-day struggle for survival, and its tiny class of entrepreneurs, unsure of the future, uncertain whether to stick it out or pack up and leave. "Everyone lives uptight hoping something will happen," said store owner Donald Rodriguez. "No one knows what to do." The most obyious change in Managua over the past three years are the tiny restaurants, boutiques and bakeries that seem to have sprung up throughout the city. And the supermarkets, which have been refurbished and restocked with a cornucopia of is.S. brand-name products. Just a few years ago those same supermarkets had half-empty shelves, holding a few staples, packed in plain plastic bags. The only "special" might be a shipment of canned fruit from 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 cigarettes or fresh fruit. Middle class families earn a little extra income by converting their garages into soft-drink stands or small sundry shops. The new businesses are often short-lived. Rodriguez, who has sold televisions and other electrical appliances in the Centro Comercial Managua since 1976, has see 10 stores at the mall change hands so far this year. His own business is down drastically. In 1990 he sold an average of 100 TV sets every two months. He sold only 30 sets in the first eight months of 1993. "isnder the Sandinistas whatever we could get we sold. Inflation was so high that whoever had money spent it as ston as possible," he said. "Now we can get all the gtods we want. But no one has any money to buy them." Even more precarious is the predicament of small manufacturers. "It's ironic -- we survived a decade of Sandinismo but we may not survive this government," said Victor Gabuardi who runs a small furniture factory. "I estimate we can survive one more year. After that we're either going to have to shut down or move to another country." Five years ago Maderas Industriales Gabuardi had 20 full-time employees. Today it has four. Gabuardi estimates that his sales are down at least 75 percent since 1990. "What's worse, inflation or recession?" he asks. "I'm no economist, so all I can say is that we survived inflation -- but the recession is killing us." And it's not just store owners who are hurting. Vendors at Managua's teeming Eastern Market also complain of falling sales. The market has mushrtomed over the past three years, spreading its small stalls over several more city blocks. Many of the new vendors are former state employees, thrown out of work because of government spending cuts. Shoppers weaving their way through the labyrinth of stalls can find anything from cheap clothing to ceiling fans, still unassembled, in boxes piled up along a curb. Several dozen vendors along one row specialize in auto parts, new or used -- i.e., stolen. But whether because of falling demand or added competition or both, the vendors at the Eastern Market are as pessimistic as the merchants at the Managua mall. "Bad," says Olga Cruz, when asked how her business was going. "Malisimo." Cruz has worked 45 of her 75 years in the market. Her stall was filled with hand-woven baskets and straw brtoms, plastic mats and coils of nylon rope. But little of it was being sold. "No one has any money to spend," she said. "There's no work and no money." Like most market women, Cruz was anti-Sandinista. During the years of hyperinflation the Front attacked the vendors of the Eastern Market as speculators, because of their resistance to price controls. Market women were dubbed the "la burguesia del delantal" -- the "aproned bourgeoisie." But now she remembers the Sandinista years with some nostalgia. "Before at least you could sell," she said. "You could survive. Now -- who knows what will happen?" Caption: photo: Violeta Chamorro.

NTT's Cutbacks Signal isncertain Transition in Japan: Economy and ... Schlesinger, Jacob;Hamilton, David Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Feb 26, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. B4 — CORPORATE FOCisS By Jacob M. Schlesinger And David P. Hamilton Staff Reporters of Ton Wall Stiif.kt Journal TOKYO - When Nippon Telegraph & Telephone Corp. announces today that It will slash staff and close offices, It will spotlight a major economic struggle under­way In Japan: the bumpy transition from cozy, controlled stability to the more uncertain free market. Japan's huge part-public, part-private telecommunications centaur - under at­tack by new competitors but tightly reined in by regulators, publicly traded but ma­jority-owned by the government - will un­veil a new cost-cutting plan likely to trim its payroll by more than 10%, or 30,000 employees, as well as a consolidation of many offices and retail outlets over the next three years, "The market struggle Is getting harder and harder," says an NTT spokesman. "We must become more efficient." Critics say NTT's new plan will barely scratch the surface. "In the long run, they will have to cut more," says Sachio Sem- moto, who quit NTT about a decade ago out of frustration with the bureaucratic com­pany and who now helps run one of its biggest competitors, DDI Corp. "Other­wise they will not survive," The catalyst for NTT's actions is tum­bling profits. The company estimates its pretax profit in the year ending March 31 will drop 35% from a year ago to 229 billion yen ($1.95 billion). NTT says that number could fall by another one-third from this year's depressed level. NTT, which had revenue last fiscal year of more than six trillion yen, is so big that its tremors are likely to hurt companies throughout this country. The company is Japan's largest employer, its biggest capital spender, and accounts for an estimated 3% of the Tokyo stock market's total capitalization. Clearly, the lackluster state of Japan's economy is one big cause of NTT's pain. But at the heart of the company's troubles is a simple fact: It was never designed to run efficiently, by free-market standards. In addition to providing basic phone serv­ices, the company has long been Japan Inc.'s sugar daddy, doling out technologi­cal gtodies and big contracts to other corporations, lavish contributions to politi­cians, and high-paying jobs to workers in the telecommunications union. in a deregulated and increasingly in­ternational market, NTT is finding it more difficult to balance those missions with the bottom line. In addition to cutting em­ployees, "now NTT Is putting severe requirements on its suppliers, telling us we have to cut prices because NTT's profits are bad," complains an executive at one supplier, NTT played a big role nurturing its main suppliers - NEC Corp., Fujitsu Ltd., Hitachi Ltd., and Okl Electric Indus­try Co.-by giving them a steady stream of contract business and paying inflated prices for it. In fac

[...TRUNCATED...]

on of small regional companies later grabbed nearly 40% of the market. Regulators: Just Crying Wolf NTT complains that government regu­lators are limiting its ability to fight back. The telecommunications ministry won't allow NTT to raise new revenue by in­creasing its local phone rates-a remarka­bly cheap 10 yen (8.5 cents) for three minutes-or to compete more effectively in long-distance by cutting rates as much as it wants. But the regulators seem to think NTT is crying wolf. Some dismiss the company's dire statements as grandstanding, and say the personnel cuts are just clever publicrelations moves meant to convince the public that NTT deserves its rate rise. Today's restructuring Is also part of a bigger battle over NTT's ultimate fat<v since the ministry still wants to trim the fat and break up the company in 1995, mirror­ing the breakup of American Telephone & Telegraph Co. In the is.S. As it is, the company pays salaries closer to Japan's top-notch banks than to manufacturers. Rather than easing out workers whose jobs became obsolete be­cause of automation, NTT opened a slew of unnecessary sales offices and staffed them with the redundant employees. Even the latest round of cuts will likely be handled as gently as possible. Wiitfe there may be an early retirement plan, the NTT spokesman says there won't be any layoffs. As an NTT planning manager in the early 1980s, DDI's Mr. Semmoto says he once floated the idea of cutting the com­pany's work force down to 160,000 em­ployees. "Of course, they cannot cut, so they transfer their employees to their subsidiaries," he says, noting that the subsidiaries bear the added costs by charg­ing more to their NTT contracts. Many of the employees affected by the latest reduc­tions will also likely end up at NTT affili­ates. • As if NTT management's job wasn't tough enough, there's one other group to placate: the long-suffering shareholders. When the company went public in 1987, Japanese citizens eagerly bought the stock, and the share price soared in the. first few months. Many critics later ar­gued, however, that the initial public offer:.. ing was rigged. The powerful finance,, ministry, they say, convinced securities;- houses to hype the stock: The brokerages); got their high fixed commissions, and the" government raked in the proceeds from the- mammoth sale. NTT's shares couldn't defy gravity for'- long, however, and crashed even before';; the broader market did. The stock is now'1 trading at 80% below its peak. Thosev shareholders who invested their life sav-'u ings In NTT shares may get some satisfac-;; tion from the fact that they're not alone:-;- The government, which arguably created;; the mess in the first place, now needs to.'i raise money, but also is loathe to dump its. NTT shares. For now, the share price has simply fallen tto far to make that worths, while. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

Vought Aircraft Industries Inc. seems to have weathered some turbulence.Now the company is just hoping for a smtoth landing.For that to happen, Vought will have to complete its transformation from a largely military-focused supplier to a company dependent on the commercial aviation industry."We're happy," said chief executive Elmer Doty, who joined the Dallas-based company in February. "Overall, the future is very bright."That optimism might have been out of place even just a few months ago at Vought, one of the most storied names in military and commercial aviation, due to major job cuts and the news that one of the company's biggest military contracts is likely to lose funding ston.But Mr. Doty said the cuts seem to be paying off, and several new projects are coming online in time to offset the potential end of the contract for C-17 planes.The two biggest new contracts are for building significant chunks of Sikorsky Aircraft Corp.'s isH-60 helicopter and Boeing Co.'s 787 airplane.The 787 Dreamliner is clearly Vought's keystone project now, with the first finished systems expected to ship early next year."When the C-17 goes away, then commercial will definitely be the lion's share of our business," Mr. Doty said. "Where Boeing goes, we go, tto."While the contract to build pieces of the C-17 plane for the military is winding down, it should be several years before that revenue dries up completely.Last month, Vought reported third-quarter sales of $354 million, up from $310 million in the third quarter of last year.The company's net loss shrank from $55 million to $13 million in the same span.Richard Aboulafia, an aerospace and defense analyst with the Teal Group, said Vought has turned a corner."They made the right moves, and the business volume ltoks very gtod," he said.But challenges remain.Some of Vought's troubles are a standard corporate tale of downsizing and cost-cutting in a competitive globaleconomy."This business was set up as a cost-plus defense business," Mr. Doty said. The company "was not well suited for a more dynamic, competitive world."Additionally, the executives who would have to spearhead that change were not trained to turn on a dime."They had never worked anywhere else," Mr. Doty said. "Their background was such that change was going to occur slowly, and we didn't have time for slow."About 1,000 workers - or about a sixth of the workforce - have been let go since last year, mostly managerial and administrative staff, and other costs have been trimmed.Vought has also started taking advantage of the terms in some of its contracts with Boeing, its largest customer. The contracts contain provisions that allow Vought to raise the prices of individual parts if Boeing orders fewer parts than originally promised.Mr. Doty said Vought had not taken advantage of those provisions before he arrived. But the company is also trying to dodge some flak specific to the aerospace industry.History of changesVought is a mishmash of previous comp

[...TRUNCATED...]

ught some lift, he said.Rtom to growMr. Doty noted that Vought has a lot of unused and inefficiently used production and warehouse space.A tour of the company's 5.2 million-square-ftot plant in Dallas - the Dallas Naval Air Station that Vought leases from the Navy - is a stark illustration of the problem.Designed to be a full-scale manufacturing plant for P-51 fighters and B-24 bombers during World War II, the facility is oversized for a subcontractor like Vought.Scores of hangars sit empty or are used for storing rusty parts. And even in buildings that are in use, some sections are nearly vacant.That's not to say it's a ghost town.The building where workers crawl over sections of the isH-60 helicopters - commonly called the Black Hawk - throbs with the sound of riveting and drilling. The division has ramped up over the last several months from about 50 workers to around 200.Vought currently has about 3,400 workers in the Dallas area.Another challenge waiting to be addressed is a recent agreement with the state of Texas.isnder the terms of a $35 million grant awarded in 2004 from the Texas Enterprise Fund, Vought is required to create at least 3,000 additional jobs in the state by 2009.Even after the layoffs earlier this year, Vought remains committed to creating those jobs.Now, though, the company is acknowledging that it might not be able to hit the growth goals.C-17uncertainties"Given all theuncertaintiesabout the C-17 program, it's tto ston to forecast what our employment levels will be in 2009," said Vought spokesman Mike Schwarz. "However, if Vought hasn't met the hiring goals of the agreement, we fully intend to abide by its terms, which include provisions for repayment."While the 787 project will likely become the single largest project at Vought, that work is done at a facility in South Carolina.But Mr. Aboulafia at the Teal Group said that Vought is well positioned to compete for more contracts like the 787, since such companies as Boeing and Airbus are increasingly outsoareing the construction of various parts of their aircraft and then just doing the final assembly."They just want to be the guy at the top integrating someone else's metal bending, or composite bending," he said.E-mail vgodinez@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (1-4. content_for_eval: President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, defending the unpopular pro-market policies he once demonized but which have marked his first year in office, said on Thursday that the sacrifices Brazilians had made this year would be repaid with sustained economic growth. Summing up his first 12 months as president, Mr. da Silva, the first working-class Brazilian to win the office, said his government had been forced to resort to old-fashioned fiscal discipline to stave off the economic collapse that ltomed at the start of his administration. Panicked by Mr. da Silva's past as a fiery labor leader, traders sent Brazilian bonds and the currency tumbling last fall. Banks shut off credit lines to companies, leaving the country all but dependent on support from the International Monetary Fund. When Mr. da Silva ttok office Jan. 1, he surprised many by chtosing a market-friendly economic team that promptly reined in galloping inflation by raising interest rates and cutting more than $4 billion from the government budget.
